segments.s721-g9: که here introduces the question/clause ('what/that who'); دارای دارنده یزدان is a poetic epithet meaning 'the one endowed with/possessed by Yazdān (God)' — two related nouns used as an honorific; چه کرد is the interrogative 'what did (he) do', with کرد (past tense of کردن) as the main verb placed at the end in typical SOV order.
segments.s721-g10: ز دشمن 'from the enemy' (ز = from, poetic); چگونه 'how'; برآورد is used here in the sense of effecting/bringing about or getting rid of (a poetic use of برآوردن, 'to accomplish/bring off'); گرد is an archaic/poetic verbal particle (related to گشت) that helps form the past/resultive sense — together برآورد گرد = 'he effected/caused to be removed' / 'he drove off'.
segments.s721-g11: به قیصر 'to Caesar' (به = to/for); یکی نامه 'a letter' (indefinite object comes before the verb); بنوشت is the archaic past 3rd‑person form 'wrote' (verb appears before the subject here, a common poetic inversion); شاه is the subject placed after the verb: 'the king wrote a letter to Caesar.'
segments.s721-g12: چناچون بود = 'it was thus/just so' (چناچون = thus/just as; بود = was); درخور پیشگاه = 'worthy/appropriate for the presence (of the court)' where درخور is a predicative adjective 'fit for' and پیشگاه = 'the royal presence/court'. The phrase qualifies how the letter was written.
segments.s721-g13: به یک هفته is a temporal/durational phrase ('for a week') placed before the verb. مجلس is the object/nominal of the action ('an assembly, gathering'), and بیاراستند is the 3rd-person plural past verb ('they prepared/arranged, literally "they adorned"). The verb at the end gives the main past action modified by the preceding time phrase.
segments.s721-g14: بهر introduces a purpose or beneficiary ('for / so that / toward'). برزنی here functions as the place or (more likely) the proper name Barzin in dative — i.e. the one who is to go. رود is a present/subjunctive 3rd-person singular of رفتن ('that he go'), used inside a desiderative clause governed by the following می‌خواستند ('they wished/wanted'); the conjunction و simply links the clauses. The whole segment expresses a purpose/intent: 'that he should go to Barzin/the plain.'
segments.s721-g15: به آتشکده is a prepositional phrase ('to the fire-temple') giving the destination for فرستاد ('sent'). هم is an adverb meaning 'also/even', placed before the verb to add emphasis. چیز is an indefinite direct object ('something'); word order here (prepositional phrase + adverb + verb + object) is natural in classical Persian.
segments.s721-g16: بران is an archaic particle meaning 'upon/onto/for them' and introduces the indirect object (those who receive the action). موبدان is the plural of موبد ('mōbed, Zoroastrian priest'), the recipients. خلعت افگند is a conventional verbal phrase: خلعت ('robe of honour') is the object and افگند (past 3rd-person singular of افکندن) here means 'put on/ bestowed' — i.e. 'bestowed the robe.' نیز at the end is another 'also/likewise' reinforcing the action. The sequence is an object (بران موبدان) + verb phrase (خلعت افگند).
segments.s721-g1: This is a nominal phrase (a personal name) followed by a verb phrase with the preposition به + person indicating the destination/recipient. چُوخراد برزین functions as the name (apposition of two name elements), then به خسرو ('to Khosrow') marks the goal, and رسید is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb 'arrived/reached'. Word order is subject — prepositional phrase — verb.
segments.s721-g2: بگفت is the simple past 3rd‑person 'he said / he told' introducing a reported clause. آن کجا ... is a compact noun clause functioning as the direct object: آن ('that/there') + کجا ('where / there') followed by a series of past verbs (کرد, دید, شنید) joined by و in coordinate parallelism. The verbs are all past tense, giving 'what he did, saw, and heard (there)'.
segments.s721-g3: دل شاه پرویز is a possessive/genitive noun phrase 'the heart of King Parviz' (noun + title + name). ازان is a causal/adverbial 'from that / because of that' referring back to the previous fact, and شاد شد is adjective + past auxiliary literally 'became glad' (شاد = glad; شد = became).
segments.s721-g4: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which / by which' linking this clause to the prior reason. بد گهر is a compound attributive ('bad / ill gem') modifying the following noun; دشمن is the subject 'the enemy', and آزاد شد is adjective + past auxiliary meaning 'became free' or 'was freed'. The construction آزاد + شد expresses the change-of-state 'was freed' rather than an active transitive voice.
segments.s721-g5: به درویش is an indirect‑object prepositional phrase 'to/for the dervish' (به + noun). بخشید is past 3rd‑person of بخشیدن used here in the sense 'gave/bestowed', and چندی درم is a quantified noun phrase 'some dirhams' (چندی = some/several preceding the coin noun).
segments.s721-g6: ز پوشیدنیها is a poetic use of the preposition ز (= از) meaning 'from/out of (his) garments' with پوشیدنیها the plural 'wearables/garments'. و از بیش وکم is an idiomatic coordination 'and from more and less' (از + بیش و کم) meaning 'and various small extras / a little of this and that'. The sequence lists different kinds of alms/gifts.
segments.s721-g7: بهر here is the preposition 'for/on behalf of'. پادشاهی is a nominal 'kingship/kingdom' (object of بهر), و connects to خودکامه‌ای, an adjectival/noun form meaning 'the autocratic (one)' or 'autocrat' — together the phrase expresses the intended beneficiary/purpose of the action.
segments.s721-g8: نوشتند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they wrote'. بر پهلوی here is a prepositional phrase indicating language/script or medium ('in Pahlavi'—Pahlavi being the middle‑Persian language/form), and نامه‌ای is the indefinite object 'a letter'. Word order: verb follows the prepositional phrase and object.
segments.s721-g25: چو here is a temporal subordinating particle meaning "when." بهرام is the subject name. باشد is the classical third-person present/subjunctive copula here functioning as "is" (literary). به دشت is a prepositional phrase "on/in the plain/into the plain," and نبرد is a noun "battle; fighting" that combines with the phrase to mean "in/at the plain of battle." Together the segment forms a temporal clause: "When Bahram is on the plain of battle..."
segments.s721-g26: کزو is a relative/adverbial compound meaning "from which / from whom." ترک is the noun "Turk" (the agent). پیرش is پیر + -ش (possessive) here meaning "his elder/old man" (an elder/leader or old age, used idiomatically). برآورد is a literary perfective/preterital verb meaning "brought out / caused to appear / produced" (in older usage it can mean "bring forth" or "cause to be"), and گرد after it marks the idea of surrounding/about (poetic ordering). The clause therefore says that the Turk "brought forth/raised his elder and (was) about/around (him)," with the relative کزو linking this action back to the preceding temporal clause.
segments.s721-g27: همه موبدان is a noun phrase "all the mobeds/priestly class." خواندند is the past-tense third-person plural verb "they recited/they cried/sang" (here used for vocal acclamation), and آفرین is an interjection meaning "Afarin! / Bravo!" The order is Subject (همه موبدان) + Verb (خواندند) + Object/interjection (آفرین), describing their shouted praise.
segments.s721-g28: که introduces reported content "that / saying that." بی تو is a prepositional/privative phrase "without you." مبیناد is an older-form verbal/impersonal construction meaning "would appear / would be seen" (a present/subjunctive-type form used in poetry). کهتر زمین is the predicate "the earth lesser/inferior" with کهتر (comparative/inferior) qualifying زمین (earth/land). The whole subordinate clause expresses what the mobeds proclaimed: that without you the earth would not be seen as lesser.
segments.s721-g29: چو + بهرام is a simile/comparison "as/like Bahram." باد here is the optative particle "may it be / would that" (an archaic/poetic use of باد). آنک is a relative particle "who/that" introducing the following relative clause, and با مهر تو is a prepositional phrase "with your favour/kindness." So the segment expresses a wish: "May he be like Bahram, who, with your favour..."
segments.s721-g30: نخواهد که is a negative volitive/intent construction: نخواهد (will not/would not wish) + که (that) introduces the content of the desire or lack thereof. رخشان is a predicate adjective "radiant/bright," بود is the copula "be," and چهر تو is the noun phrase "your face." Word order here is poetic (adjective + copula + noun) but reads as "would not wish that your face be radiant."
segments.s721-g17: This is a compact, poetic word order: the name phrase (بخراد برزین) appears before the verb, then the adverb چنین and the verb گفت, with the subject شاه placed after the verb (verb–subject order common in epic poetry). Read as “to Bakhrad Barzin (بخراد برزین) thus (چنین) said the king (گفت شاه).”
segments.s721-g18: که begins a subordinate clause (here introducing the content of what was said: a conditional question). زیبد is a third‑person present form meaning “befit / suit” (impersonal/anticipatory use). تو را is the object/dative “you” with the enclitic object marker; راگر is actually را + گر (= 'to you, if') and functions as the conditional marker “if” attached after the object: تو را گر دهم = “if I give (it) to you.” تاج و گاه = “crown and (throne/seat)” (poetic pair).
segments.s721-g19: دهانش = دهان + enclitic ­ـش “his mouth”; پر از = a construction meaning “full of”; گوهر شاهوار = “gems” modified by the adjective شاهوار (“regal/lofty”). Word order is noun+possessive then predicate phrase with پر از + noun+adjective.
segments.s721-g20: This continues the description: the verb or verbal sense is elliptical/poetic here; بیاگند is the verb form present in the text but its exact semantic fit is obscure in modern glosses (see note). و = “and”; دینار = “dinar(s), money”; چون introduces a comparison “as / like” (چون صد هزار = “as if a hundred thousand”).
segments.s721-g21: همی‌ریخت is the imperfectative/past‑progressive poetic form meaning “was pouring / kept pouring.” گنجور here functions as the treasure/treasury subject (poetic adjective/noun meaning ‘treasure‑laden’/‘treasury’). در پای اوی = locative phrase “at his feet,” with اوی the possessive/third‑person clitic “his.”
segments.s721-g22: برین گونه = بر + این + گونه, an archaic/poetic phrasing meaning “in this manner / thus.” تا introduces the limit/time “until”; تنگ شد is a two‑word predicate “became tight / cramped” (شد = became); جای اوی = “his place” (جای = place, اوی = his).
segments.s721-g23: بدو is the dative phrase “to him / unto him.” گفت is the saying verb (here 'said'); in this line it governs a relative/conditional clause. هرکس که پیچد ز راه = “whoever (هرکس) who (که) turns aside/turns (پیچد) from (ز) the road (راه).” پیچد is present subjunctive/indefinite used for generic conditional meaning “turns aside.”
segments.s721-g24: This is a compact jussive/optative curse: شود + noun phrase expresses a wish or consequence “may … become.” روز روشن = “bright day” (literal subject); برو بر سیاه functions idiomatically as ‘turn/come to black’ — i.e. “become black/dark.” The verb شود is third‑person jussive/optative here (‘may it become’), and the rest names what becomes black.

sentences.s721-s5: What did the God‑endowed possessor do, and how did he drive the enemy away?
sentences.s721-s6: The king wrote a letter to Caesar, in a manner fitting for the royal presence.
sentences.s721-s7: They set up an assembly for a week, wishing that Barzin should go.
sentences.s721-s8: He also sent something to the fire-temple, and likewise bestowed robes upon those mobeds.
sentences.s721-s1: When Chukhrad Barzin reached Khosrow, he told him what he there had done, seen, and heard.
sentences.s721-s2: King Parviz's heart became glad at that, for by it the enemy was freed from the ill (or unlucky) gem.
sentences.s721-s3: He gave the dervish some dirhams, and (gave) garments and a little of surplus and deficiency.
sentences.s721-s4: They wrote a letter in Pahlavi on behalf of kingship and the autocrat.
sentences.s721-s13: When Bahram is on the plain in battle, the Turk brings forth his elder and circles him about.
sentences.s721-s14: All the mobeds cried 'Afarin!' saying that without you the earth would not seem diminished.
sentences.s721-s15: May he be like Bahram — one who, with your favour, would not wish that your face be more radiant.
sentences.s721-s9: The king said thus to Bakhrad Barzin: "Would it befit you if I gave you the crown and the throne?"
sentences.s721-s10: His mouth was full of regal gems, and dinars as if a hundred thousand.
sentences.s721-s11: Treasure kept pouring at his feet in this fashion until his place became cramped.
sentences.s721-s12: He said to him: “Whoever turns aside from the road — may his bright day turn black.”
